channel.title: TechNode
channel.description: Latest news and trends about tech in China

item.title: When toys learn to talk, AiSpea turns all things into AI-powered companions
item.dc:creator: Jessie Wu
item.description: As children, many of us imagined our toys coming to life—speaking, thinking, and developing personalities of their own. Today, that long-held fantasy is being transformed into reality by Chinese technology company AiSpea. The company’s latest product, All-Things Party (万物派对), aims to imbue everyday objects with AI, effectively giving them a digital soul. By integrating AI […]
item.content:encoded:
As children, many of us imagined our toys coming to life—speaking, thinking, and developing personalities of their own. Today, that long-held fantasy is being transformed into reality by Chinese technology company AiSpea.

The company’s latest product, All-Things Party (万物派对), aims to imbue everyday objects with AI, effectively giving them a digital soul. By integrating AI and interactive technologies, the platform enables ordinary items to become responsive companions capable of engaging in dialogue, expressing emotion, and offering personalized interaction.

A dream-building team emerges from Zhejiang University’s Lab

AiSpea’s R&D team brings together talent from top institutions including Zhejiang University and IBM, combining expertise in large-scale AI models, hardware circuit design, and even in-house mobile app development. 

Long before AI-powered gadgets became trendy, the team had already set their sights on children’s education and companionship. By pairing AI with toys, they carved out a niche of their own. Today, their AI-powered plush toys and desktop robots are selling overseas.

All-Things Party: the AI magic that brings objects to life

At the heart of All-Things Party is a simple but powerful idea: giving everyday objects an AI-powered soul. Users place their toys, figurines, or even a water bottle or lipstick onto AiSpea’s AI base, and then use a dedicated mobile app to customize each character’s personality, voice, and conversational style. 

Teddy bear might turn into a master of cheesy jokes, while water bottle could become a health-obsessed chatterbox, constantly reminding users to stay hydrated.

What makes it even more fun is that these AI characters don’t just talk to users — they can also talk to each other. Users can invite multiple characters into a group chat and watch them banter, collaborate, or even co-create a story together.

This smooth, multi-character interaction is powered by Alibaba Cloud’s Qwen large language model. Behind the scenes, the model accurately understands conversational intent, keeps track of context, and manages the logic and turn-taking of responses.

From toys to parallel universes, where AI unlocks endless possibilities

AiSpea’s ambitions extend well beyond toys. The company has partnered with Youku to bring characters from the hit web drama The Young Brewmaster’s Adventure onto its AI platform. Fans can now hold real-time, face-to-face conversations with their favorite on-screen personalities.

Looking ahead, these AI characters could be connected to smart home systems, acting as digital assistants that control lighting and music. They could also move into educational settings, where they would serve as AI tutors, accompanying children as they learn English and other subjects.

As AiSpea CEO Zhou Yixu puts it, “We want to put the power of interaction back into users’ hands.” In the world of All-Things Party, every object can become part of a parallel universe created by users — and users are the ultimate creator of this ever-expanding digital playground.

item.title: Xiaomi’s SU7 Ultra enters Gran Turismo 7, marking first Chinese car in flagship racing game
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Xiaomi’s flagship electric vehicle, the SU7 Ultra, has been added to Sony’s hit racing game Gran Turismo 7, becoming the first car from a Chinese brand to appear in the long-running franchise, the company said on Wednesday. Xiaomi founder Lei Jun said in a promotional video that the move aims to give global players a […]
item.content:encoded: Xiaomi’s flagship electric vehicle, the SU7 Ultra, has been added to Sony’s hit racing game Gran Turismo 7, becoming the first car from a Chinese brand to appear in the long-running franchise, the company said on Wednesday. Xiaomi founder Lei Jun said in a promotional video that the move aims to give global players a chance to experience the performance and handling of China’s high-end smart vehicles. The SU7 Ultra is equipped with a tri-motor electric powertrain producing a combined 1,548 horsepower and can accelerate from zero to 100 km/h in 1.98 seconds. The model last year set a new production-car lap record at the Shanghai International Circuit, completing the track in 2 minutes 9.944 seconds, according to Xiaomi. [Technode Reporting]

item.title: From Biscuits to Bytes: Inside the Lisbon Factory Breeding Global Unicorns
item.dc:creator: TechNode Staff
item.description: When our team pulled into Lisbon’s Beato Innovation District, we didn’t find the sterile glass skyscrapers typical of modern tech hubs. Instead, we were greeted by red-brick industrial buildings with a strong sense of history—structures rooted in heritage, free from cold corporate uniformity. José, Director of Unicorn Factory Lisboa, stood amid the complex and pointed […]
item.content:encoded:
When our team pulled into Lisbon’s Beato Innovation District, we didn’t find the sterile glass skyscrapers typical of modern tech hubs. Instead, we were greeted by red-brick industrial buildings with a strong sense of history—structures rooted in heritage, free from cold corporate uniformity.

José, Director of Unicorn Factory Lisboa, stood amid the complex and pointed out the history etched into the skyline. “That used to be a cookie and pasta factory,” he explained, gesturing to the surrounding buildings. A former bread factory nearby is under renovation, and a silent milling plant still stands—remnants of an era when this 35,000-square-meter site supplied food to Lisbon. Today, it has a new mission: fueling the future of Europe’s digital economy.

A Cathedral for Innovators

Launched in 2017, the Unicorn Factory paused briefly during the pandemic but has since been revitalized. José notes with pride that 10 of the 18 heritage buildings have been renovated and put into use, with over 1,300 innovators working here. This is far more than a coworking space—it is a unique sanctuary where major deep-tech companies, startups and research centers coexist and collaborate.

Our cameras captured the “hardcore” reality of this ecosystem:

  • Micro Harvest has more than just office desks—it operates a compact bio-factory right inside the building, using bacteria to cultivate protein for sustainable fish feed, and has already achieved large-scale production.
  • Claranet, a cybersecurity firm recently acquired by a telecom giant, has built a top-tier situational awareness room for its 500 employees in the old factory, along with a dedicated R&D and training center for cybersecurity.

An Insight into Global Expansion

Gil Azevedo, Executive Director of Unicorn Factory Lisboa, shared the scale of their efforts: in just one year, 250 startups and over 1,000 students have joined their programs, with half of the startups coming from outside Portugal.

In our in-depth conversation, Gil shared an interesting observation: compared to countries with larger markets, where startups often wait until they scale up to pursue global expansion, Portuguese businesses start seeking international opportunities at a very early stage. For them, overseas expansion is an essential requirement for growth.

This trend has nurtured a thriving startup ecosystem in Portugal. The country has spawned seven unicorn companies, six of which emerged years ago, with artificial intelligence playing a key role in each of their business models. Beyond AI, the ecosystem boasts competitive enterprises in health tech, food tech and fintech. Portugal also leads Europe in green economy innovation within the sustainability sector, and even traditional industries like textiles are being reshaped by innovative solutions.

A Hub Connecting Three Continents

To support such growth, the Unicorn Factory has built a comprehensive six-pillar support system, covering youth entrepreneurship, early-stage incubation, scale-up programs, innovation centers, international collaboration and social innovation.

As Gil emphasizes, Portugal is emerging as a unique global hub connecting innovators across three continents. With a solid talent pool and inclusive multicultural environment, it serves not only as a springboard to the European market but also as a natural bridge to South America (especially Brazil) and Africa. Drawing on its open spirit dating back to the Age of Discovery, this former factory has become a truly international stage for innovation. “Our vision is to achieve true globalization,” Gil said.

Join the Odyssey

Through our lenses, we will take you between historic industrial relics and cutting-edge tech labs, where pioneers will share how they transformed abandoned factories into a top European incubator, and reveal the “Third Option” this hub offers to global innovators.

Let’s open this door and discover a different side of Portugal.

Stay tuned to TechNode for the premiere of our new series, Tech Odyssey.

item.title: Alibaba founder Jack Ma says children should be taught how to use AI, not whether to use it
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Speaking online at an event hosted by a rural education charity foundation, Jack Ma said AI posed challenges for rural education but also offered an opportunity to return to the core purpose of teaching. “In the AI era, the question is no longer whether to use AI, but how to teach our children to use […]
item.content:encoded:
Speaking online at an event hosted by a rural education charity foundation, Jack Ma said AI posed challenges for rural education but also offered an opportunity to return to the core purpose of teaching.

“In the AI era, the question is no longer whether to use AI, but how to teach our children to use it well,” Ma said. He added that education should no longer push students to compete with machines in calculation and memorisation, but instead nurture curiosity, imagination, creativity, judgment and collaboration. “The real divide in the AI age is not a technological gap, but a gap in curiosity, imagination and creativity,” he said.

Ma said education should encourage students to think more creatively and independently, rather than produce standardised answers. “It should not be about 1,000 students giving the same correct answer, but about learning to ask 10,000 good questions,” he said. [Technode Reporting]

item.title: DeepSeek releases OCR 2 with new visual encoding architecture, targeting more human-like machine vision
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Chinese AI startup DeepSeek on Tuesday released a research paper and open-sourced its latest optical character recognition (OCR) model, DeepSeek-OCR 2, aiming to improve how machines interpret and process visual information. The company said the model is built on its DeepEncoder V2 architecture, which replaces rigid scanning-based visual encoding with a semantic reasoning approach, enabling […]
item.content:encoded:
Chinese AI startup DeepSeek on Tuesday released a research paper and open-sourced its latest optical character recognition (OCR) model, DeepSeek-OCR 2, aiming to improve how machines interpret and process visual information. The company said the model is built on its DeepEncoder V2 architecture, which replaces rigid scanning-based visual encoding with a semantic reasoning approach, enabling AI systems to rearrange image components dynamically according to context and meaning.

DeepSeek said the model improves data compression efficiency and needs only 256 to 1,120 visual tokens to process complex document pages, cutting computational costs for downstream large language models. In benchmark tests on OmniDocBench v1.5, DeepSeek-OCR 2 achieved an overall score of 91.09%, a 3.73% improvement over the previous generation, with strong performance in reading order recognition.

The release comes as Chinese AI developers intensify efforts to improve foundational models and open-source capabilities, amid growing competition in large language models and multimodal AI systems. [Technode Reporting]

item.title: Ubisoft’s Tom Clancy’s Rainbow Six Siege approved for China
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: On Monday, Ubisoft’s tactical shooter Tom Clancy’s Rainbow Six Siege, operated in China by Tencent, was approved, making it one of five imported games cleared in the first batch of 2026 licenses. For January, 182 games were approved, including 177 domestic titles. The game, known for its 5v5 asymmetric attack-and-defense gameplay, full-environment destruction mechanics, and […]
item.content:encoded: On Monday, Ubisoft’s tactical shooter Tom Clancy’s Rainbow Six Siege, operated in China by Tencent, was approved, making it one of five imported games cleared in the first batch of 2026 licenses. For January, 182 games were approved, including 177 domestic titles. The game, known for its 5v5 asymmetric attack-and-defense gameplay, full-environment destruction mechanics, and high-tech equipment such as breaching tools and drones, has built a large global fanbase. Tencent will handle localization, voice-over work, and preparation for domestic esports events ahead of its launch in China. Tencent has not yet announced a specific launch date for the Chinese market. [Technode Reporting]

item.title: AI gets social in China – Tencent tests Yuanbao Groups for AI-powered social interaction
item.dc:creator: Jessie Wu
item.description: On Monday, Tencent’s AI assistant Yuanbao rolled out a new social feature, Yuanbao Groups (元宝派), in beta testing, aiming to deepen the integration of AI into multi-user social interactions and expand real-world use cases. Users can create or join groups and invite friends via WeChat and QQ to experience AI-powered group chats, collaborative tasks, and interactive entertainment activities. […]
item.content:encoded:
On Monday, Tencent’s AI assistant Yuanbao rolled out a new social feature, Yuanbao Groups (元宝派), in beta testing, aiming to deepen the integration of AI into multi-user social interactions and expand real-world use cases. Users can create or join groups and invite friends via WeChat and QQ to experience AI-powered group chats, collaborative tasks, and interactive entertainment activities.

Currently, AI applications have struggled with low user retention and limited usage scenarios. Tencent’s move shows growing efforts among Chinese tech companies to embed AI into social and collaborative settings, potentially shifting competition in the sector from tool-based functions toward scenario-driven applications.

Details: Tencent is taking AI into its comfort zone — social networking — using Yuanbao Groups to see how AI can actually help in group chats, like making things more efficient and adding a sense of companionship, while also testing ideas that could later be built into WeChat.

  • Unlike traditional chatbots built around one-on-one interactions, Yuanbao Groups positions AI as a social lubricant that meets practical needs and also responds to people’s desire for emotional connection.
  • Combining social networking and AI, users can @Yuanbao within a Yuanbao Group to ask questions, summarize chats, or set reminders. The AI can also help lighten the mood in conversations or start tasks like fitness and study check-ins.
  • For fun and collaboration, users can share their screens and hold online meetings. Soon, there will be Watch Together and Listen Together features so people can watch movies, listen to music, and chat about it in real time.
  • Users can connect across platforms, with deep integration between WeChat and QQ, two of China’s most popular messaging and social apps. They can invite friends to join by sharing a link or a group ID.

Context: On Jan. 25, Tencent said that starting February 1, it will give out one billion yuan ($140 million) in cash red envelopes through the Yuanbao app. The move ties in with the Lunar New Year to get people involved and also drives traffic to Yuanbao Groups.

  • Tencent’s Yuanbao app was launched in May 2024 as Tencent’s standalone AI assistant application built on its Hunyuan large language model.

item.title: Unitree named robot partner for 2026 Spring Festival Gala
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Chinese robotics firm Unitree Robotics said on Monday it has been named a robot partner for China Media Group’s 2026 Spring Festival Gala, marking the company’s third collaboration with the country’s most-watched annual television event. Unitree first appeared at the gala in 2021, when its robotic ox Benben debuted during the Year of the Ox celebrations. In […]
item.content:encoded:
Chinese robotics firm Unitree Robotics said on Monday it has been named a robot partner for China Media Group’s 2026 Spring Festival Gala, marking the company’s third collaboration with the country’s most-watched annual television event. Unitree first appeared at the gala in 2021, when its robotic ox Benben debuted during the Year of the Ox celebrations. In 2025, the company’s humanoid robots featured in a performance titled Yang BOT, directed by Chinese filmmaker Zhang Yimou.

The announcement comes as competition among Chinese robotics firms intensifies, with several companies seeking high-profile exposure through the gala. On Jan. 25, Galbot Co. Ltd. said it had become the designated embodied large-model robot partner for the 2026 event. Two days earlier,  MagicLab announced it had been named the gala’s strategic intelligent robot partner for the Year of the Horse. [Technode Reporting]

item.title: Hypergryph’s Arknights: Endfield suspends PayPal payments after launch-day glitch
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Arknights: Endfield launched globally on Jan. 22, but Chinese game developer Hypergryph said it has suspended PayPal payments after a payment system malfunction on the first day led to inconsistencies between customer orders and in-game item delivery. The company said in a statement on its official X account that the issue affected a limited number […]
item.content:encoded: Arknights: Endfield launched globally on Jan. 22, but Chinese game developer Hypergryph said it has suspended PayPal payments after a payment system malfunction on the first day led to inconsistencies between customer orders and in-game item delivery. The company said in a statement on its official X account that the issue affected a limited number of transactions made via PayPal through its official launcher and on its top-up website, topup.gryphline.com. Hypergryph has disabled PayPal as a payment option, conducted a full review of all PayPal transactions, identified the affected orders, and issued full refunds. The company said PayPal payments would only be reinstated once the issue has been fully resolved and thoroughly tested. [IThome, in Chinese]

item.title: From “Made in China” to “Created for the Globe”: TechNode Launches the 2026 Born Global Awards
item.dc:creator: TechNode Staff
item.description: For years, Chinese hardware companies were best known in global markets for contract manufacturing and cost efficiency. Today, a growing number of them are designing, building, and selling their own products overseas, competing on technology, product experience, and brand rather than price alone. From robotic lawn mowers used in European households to 3D printers serving […]
item.content:encoded:
For years, Chinese hardware companies were best known in global markets for contract manufacturing and cost efficiency. Today, a growing number of them are designing, building, and selling their own products overseas, competing on technology, product experience, and brand rather than price alone.

From robotic lawn mowers used in European households to 3D printers serving makers in the United States; from portable power stations supporting outdoor activities to XR devices exploring new forms of digital interaction, these companies are increasingly integrated into everyday global use cases. Many of them are built on China’s mature supply chain ecosystem, particularly in the Greater Bay Area, while targeting international consumers from the outset.

To identify and document this shift, TechNode, Asia’s most influential international technology innovation service platform, officially launches the 2026 Born Global Awards to recognize both the hardware pioneers conquering overseas markets and the ecosystem builders driving this global expansion.

Two Lists, One Focus: Global Hardware Innovation

The 2026 Born Global Awards consist of two main lists:

1. Born Global 50

Recognizing Chinese hardware companies with established overseas footholds

The Born Global 50 will honor 50 Chinese hardware companies with solid presences in global markets, including North America, Europe, Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America.

The evaluation does not rely solely on revenue scale. Instead, it focuses on three core pillars: innovation capability, overseas market performance, and independent brand development.

Key focus areas include, but are not limited to:

  • Yard and home robotics: Robotic lawn mowers, pool cleaners, snow-removal robots, and smart home security products for overseas households.
  • Creator tools and productivity hardware: 3D printers, laser engravers, panoramic cameras, and desktop manufacturing tools for global creators and professionals.
  • Green energy and mobility: Portable and residential energy storage products, e-bikes, and other electric mobility devices.
  • Smart industrial and logistics: AMRs, warehouse automation systems, and industrial inspection equipment for manufacturing and logistics operations.
  • Innovative devices and wearables: XR and spatial computing hardware, AI translation devices, exoskeletons, and emerging human-machine interface products.

Applications are also open to companies in MedTech, AgriTech, AutoTech, and other hardware sectors—provided they operate under their own brands and serve overseas markets.

2. Born Global Leaders

Recognizing contributors to the global hardware ecosystem

Global hardware expansion depends on more than just product companies. The Born Global Leaders list highlights individuals and organizations that enable and accelerate international growth, including:

Visionaries (Founders, CEOs, and product leaders)
Those responsible for defining products, leading teams, and building brands for global users.

Backers (Investors and venture capital firms)
Institutions and individuals supporting hardware companies with capital, strategic guidance, and international networks.

Bridge Builders (Platforms and service providers)
Crowdfunding platforms, logistics providers, compliance specialists, marketing agencies, and other infrastructure partners helping companies operate in overseas markets.

Selection Approach: Focus on Long-Term Capability

The 2026 Born Global Awards do not prioritize short-term scale alone. Selection is based on long-term potential and tangible performance in global markets.

For companies, key considerations include overseas revenue or user traction, ownership of independent brands, and the ability to compete through differentiated products rather than cost advantages alone.

For ecosystem leaders, the assessment focuses on measurable contributions to helping Chinese hardware companies enter and scale in international markets.

Participation Benefits

Selected companies and leaders will unlock exclusive benefits to accelerate their global growth, including but not limited to:

  • Global Visibility: Exclusive coverage on TechNode’s bilingual platforms, reaching international media, investors, and industry partners.
  • Official Recognition: “Born Global” certification serving as a trusted endorsement for partners and customers.
  • Networking Opportunities: Opportunities to engage with founders, investors, and ecosystem partners through premier industry events, including BEYOND Expo and XIN Summit.
  • Fast-Track Access: Direct advancement to the final selection phase of the 2026 BEYOND Awards for Born Global 50 honorees.

Call for Applications

TechNode invites founders, investors, and ecosystem partners to apply or nominate candidates for the 2026 Born Global Awards. Companies at all stages of global development are welcome, as long as they operate in hardware-related sectors and serve international markets.

Entry fee: Free
Application deadline: February 24, 2026

How to Apply: Please select the relevant category below to submit your application:

For Companies (Born Global 50): [Click here to apply for the Enterprise List] (Or visit: https://cn.technode.com/2026-born-global-50-enterprise-application/)

For Leaders & Ecosystem Partners (Born Global Leaders): [Click here to apply for the Leaders List] (Or visit: https://cn.technode.com/2026-born-global-leaders-application/)
